title: Integer Representation
summary: Computers store all data as binary. This lesson covers how unsigned integers are represented using positional binary notation, hexadecimal, and how bytes and words relate to computer word sizes.
content_html:
Why Binary?

Computers store data digitally as 0s and 1s. Binary is chosen because it is simple, robust, and supports the Shannon-Hartley laws. A voltage above ~2.4V = 1, below ~0.8V = 0 (TTL standard).

Bit — a single binary digit (0 or 1). The smallest unit of information.
Byte — 8 bits. The smallest addressable unit in most CPUs.
Word — the register size of the CPU (8, 16, 32, or 64 bits depending on architecture).

Positional Number System

In decimal, each digit's value depends on its position (powers of 10). Binary works the same way but uses powers of 2:

01001110₂ = 0×2⁷ + 1×2⁶ + 0×2⁵ + 0×2⁴ + 1×2³ + 1×2² + 1×2¹ + 0×2⁰ = 64+8+4+2 = 78₁₀

Binary Counting

DecimalBinaryDecimalBinary
0000081000
1000191001
20010101010
30011111011
40100121100
50101131101
60110141110
70111151111

Hexadecimal

Hex uses base 16 (digits 0-9, A-F). Each hex digit represents exactly 4 binary bits, making it a compact shorthand for binary data.

4E₁₆ = 0100 1110₂ = 64+14 = 78₁₀
HexBinaryDecimal
0-90000-10010-9
A101010
B101111
C110012
D110113
E111014
F111115
Notation: Binary: 010011102 or %01001110 or b01001110. Hex: 4E₁₆ or 0x4E or 4Eh.

Bit Operations

Bit-wise operations manipulate individual bits within a word:

OperationSymbolDescription
NOT~Flip each bit (0→1, 1→0)
AND&Both bits must be 1
OR|Either bit may be 1
XOR^Bits must differ

exercises.title: Why binary?
exercises.prompt: Why do computers use binary rather than decimal?
exercises.options_json: ["Binary is faster to type", "Binary is simple and robust using two voltage states", "Binary uses fewer electrons", "Binary was invented first"]
exercises.answer: Binary is simple and robust using two voltage states

exercises.title: Addressable unit
exercises.prompt: The smallest addressable unit in most CPUs is:
exercises.options_json: ["A bit", "A nibble (4 bits)", "A byte (8 bits)", "A word (32 bits)"]
